---
title: "08 · Decimals"
subtitle: "Place value, extended rightward across the dot"
lesson-id: "t1.decimals"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.fractions-as-parts", "t1.place-value"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["systems", "ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 25
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [Lesson 3 — Place Value](../03-place-value/index.qmd)
   and [Lesson 7 — Fractions](../07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)?
2. Can you say what each digit of **357** is worth?
3. Have you ever read a price like "Rs. 24.50" or a measurement like
   "1.6 metres"?
:::

## Touch

Set whole, tenths, and hundredths. Watch the bar fill, and read the
decimal at the bottom.

```{ojs}
viewof whole = Inputs.range([0, 9], {value: 1, step: 1, label: "Ones:"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof tenths = Inputs.range([0, 9], {value: 4, step: 1, label: "Tenths:"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof hundredths = Inputs.range([0, 9], {value: 7, step: 1, label: "Hundredths:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const value = whole + tenths/10 + hundredths/100;
  const W = 720, H = 130, pad = 20;
  const cellW = (W - 2 * pad) / 100;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px;");

  // 100 cells representing 0.00 to 1.00 in 0.01 increments — but show scale
  // Actually display: a number line from 0 to (whole+1) with the marker
  const xMin = 0, xMax = Math.max(whole + 1, 2);
  const x = v => pad + ((v - xMin) / (xMax - xMin)) * (W - 2 * pad);

  // axis
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad).attr("x2", W - pad)
    .attr("y1", H/2).attr("y2", H/2)
    .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);

  // major ticks at each whole
  for (let v = 0; v <= xMax; v++) {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", x(v)).attr("x2", x(v))
      .attr("y1", H/2 - 8).attr("y2", H/2 + 8)
      .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", x(v)).attr("y", H/2 + 24)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13").text(v);
  }
  // minor ticks at each tenth
  for (let v = 0; v <= xMax; v += 0.1) {
    if (Math.abs(v - Math.round(v)) < 1e-9) continue;
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", x(v)).attr("x2", x(v))
      .attr("y1", H/2 - 4).attr("y2", H/2 + 4)
      .attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-width", 0.7);
  }
  // marker
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", x(value)).attr("cy", H/2).attr("r", 8)
    .attr("fill", "#c2185b").attr("stroke", "#fff").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", x(value)).attr("y", H/2 - 14)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#c2185b").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(value.toFixed(2));
  return svg.node();
}
```

```{ojs}
{
  const value = whole + tenths/10 + hundredths/100;
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; font-family:monospace; font-size:1.4rem; margin-top:0.5rem;">
    ${whole} + ${tenths}/10 + ${hundredths}/100
    &nbsp;=&nbsp;<span style="font-weight:bold;">${value.toFixed(2)}</span>
  </div>`;
}
```

The dot is just a marker. To its left: place values are 1, 10, 100 (the
ones, tens, hundreds you already know). To its right: each step shrinks
the value by ten — tenths, hundredths, thousandths, ...

## Wonder

1. What does the digit "7" in **0.7** mean? What does "7" mean in **0.07**?
2. Why does **2.50** mean the same as **2.5**? What if you wrote
   **2.5000**?
3. The fraction $\tfrac{1}{4}$ is also **0.25**. The fraction $\tfrac{1}{3}$
   is...?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Read aloud: **3.7**, **0.42**, **2.05**.

What is each digit worth?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- **3.7** = "three and seven tenths" = $3 + \tfrac{7}{10}$.
- **0.42** = "zero and forty-two hundredths" = $\tfrac{4}{10} + \tfrac{2}{100}$.
- **2.05** = "two and five hundredths" = $2 + \tfrac{0}{10} + \tfrac{5}{100}$.

The 0 in the tenths column of "2.05" is doing the same placeholder duty
as the 0 in 305 (Lesson 3). Without it, you'd read "2.5", which is *ten
times bigger*.
</details>

### Problem 2

Convert these fractions to decimals: $\dfrac{1}{2}$, $\dfrac{3}{4}$,
$\dfrac{1}{5}$, $\dfrac{1}{8}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A fraction with denominator 10, 100, 1000, ... is read directly as a
decimal. Rewrite each fraction with such a denominator.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{5}{10} = 0.5$
- $\tfrac{3}{4} = \tfrac{75}{100} = 0.75$
- $\tfrac{1}{5} = \tfrac{2}{10} = 0.2$
- $\tfrac{1}{8} = \tfrac{125}{1000} = 0.125$

Every fraction whose denominator is a product of 2s and 5s converts to a
*finite* decimal.
</details>

### Problem 3

Try $\dfrac{1}{3}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Long-divide $1.000\ldots$ by $3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1 \div 3 = 0.3333\ldots$ — the 3s never stop.

We write this as $0.\overline{3}$ — the bar over the 3 means "repeats
forever". Some fractions (those whose denominator has prime factors other
than 2 or 5) give *repeating* decimals. The decimal expansion is *infinite
but periodic*. It's still a perfectly good number — just one that needs
infinitely many digits to write in decimal form.

Both $0.\overline{3}$ and $\tfrac{1}{3}$ are exactly the same number, in
two different costumes.
</details>

### Problem 4

Compute **2.4 + 1.7** by treating the decimal point as the alignment guide.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Add column by column from the right, just like whole-number addition.
Carry across the dot if needed.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$\begin{array}{r} 2.4 \\ +\,1.7 \\ \hline 4.1 \end{array}$$
Tenths: $4 + 7 = 11$ → write 1, carry 1.
Ones: $2 + 1 + 1 = 4$.

**Answer: 4.1.** Decimal arithmetic is just place-value arithmetic with
columns extending to the right of the dot.
</details>

### Problem 5

What is **0.1 × 0.1**? (Picture two strips, each one-tenth of a unit
square.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The rectangle picture from [Lesson 5](../05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)
still applies. Width 0.1, height 0.1. What fraction of a unit square is
that?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01$ — one *hundredth*. Picture: a tiny square,
one-tenth wide and one-tenth tall, fits exactly 100 times into a 1×1
square (10 across, 10 down). Each tiny square is $\tfrac{1}{100}$ of the
whole.

So multiplying two decimals less than 1 gives a *smaller* result. This
contradicts the whole-number intuition "multiplication makes things
bigger" — and is the source of much confusion. The rectangle picture
explains it cleanly.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **decimal** is a number written in **base-10 place value**, with
columns continuing to the right of a *decimal point* — but each step
right divides the value by 10 (instead of multiplying by 10 as we go
left).

The columns are:
$$\ldots \mid \text{hundreds} \mid \text{tens} \mid \text{ones} \mathbf{\ .\ } \text{tenths} \mid \text{hundredths} \mid \text{thousandths} \mid \ldots$$

A decimal is the same as a fraction:
$$0.d_1 d_2 d_3 = \frac{d_1}{10} + \frac{d_2}{100} + \frac{d_3}{1000}.$$

A fraction whose reduced denominator only has prime factors **2** and
**5** terminates as a decimal; otherwise the decimal **repeats**
forever.

:::

> *To the right of the dot, divide instead of multiply;*
> *Each new column, ten times smaller than the last.*
> *Every fraction has a decimal —*
> *Some end; some sing forever.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $2.45 + 1.6$.

A competent solver **aligns the decimal points**, padding with a zero
where needed:
$$\begin{array}{r} 2.45 \\ +\,1.60 \\ \hline 4.05 \end{array}$$
Hundredths: $5 + 0 = 5$. Tenths: $4 + 6 = 10$ — write 0, carry 1. Ones:
$2 + 1 + 1 = 4$. **Answer: 4.05.**

Padding 1.6 to "1.60" makes the place-value alignment explicit. The
trailing zero adds nothing, but it makes the column structure visible.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Convert: $\tfrac{2}{5}$, $\tfrac{7}{10}$, $\tfrac{9}{100}$ to
decimals.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0.4$, $0.7$, $0.09$.
</details>

**W2.** Convert: $0.6$, $0.25$, $0.125$ to fractions in their simplest
form.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0.6 = \tfrac{6}{10} = \tfrac{3}{5}$. $\ 0.25 = \tfrac{25}{100} =
\tfrac{1}{4}$. $\ 0.125 = \tfrac{125}{1000} = \tfrac{1}{8}$.
</details>

**W3.** Compute $3.2 - 1.7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Tenths: $2 - 7$ underflows; borrow 1 from the ones column. Tenths: $12 -
7 = 5$. Ones: $(3-1) - 1 = 1$. **Answer: 1.5.**
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Compute $0.4 \times 0.5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$0.4 \times 0.5 = \tfrac{4}{10} \times \tfrac{5}{10}$. Multiply across.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{4 \times 5}{10 \times 10} = \tfrac{20}{100} = 0.2$.

A useful **shortcut:** count the digits *after the decimal point* in each
factor (here, 1 and 1 — total 2). The product has the same total of
decimal digits. $4 \times 5 = 20$; place the decimal so the result has 2
decimal digits: 0.20 = 0.2. ✓
</details>

**P2.** Order from smallest to largest: $0.5, \ 0.05, \ 0.55, \ 0.005$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The widest fraction is the smallest. Pad with zeros to make all the same
length: $0.500, 0.050, 0.550, 0.005$. Now compare like whole numbers.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0.005 < 0.05 < 0.5 < 0.55$.

A surprisingly common error: assuming "more digits = bigger". With the
same digits but different decimal-point positions, place value rules.
</details>

**P3.** A petrol pump charges Rs. 102.40 per litre. You pump 12.5 litres.
What's the bill?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$102.40 \times 12.5$. Use round-number splitting: $102.40 \times 10 =
1024$; $102.40 \times 2 = 204.80$; $102.40 \times 0.5 = 51.20$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$102.40 \times 12.5 = 1024 + 204.80 + 51.20 = 1280$. **Rs. 1,280.**
Decimals turn "12 and a half litres at 102 rupees and 40 paise" into a
single tidy multiplication.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A computer stores numbers using a fixed number of bits — say, 32
bits. The decimal $0.1$ cannot be represented *exactly* in binary. Why
might that be? (Hint: the binary place values to the right of the dot
are $\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{4}, \tfrac{1}{8}, \tfrac{1}{16}, \ldots$ —
all powers of 2.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Recall *Discover P3*: a fraction terminates in base 10 iff its
denominator's prime factors are 2 and 5. What is the analogous condition
for base 2?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
In base 2, only fractions whose denominators are powers of 2 terminate.
$0.1 = \tfrac{1}{10} = \tfrac{1}{2 \times 5}$ — the prime factor **5**
makes it *non-terminating in binary*. So $0.1$ in binary is a *repeating
binary fraction*, like $\tfrac{1}{3}$ in our decimal system.

Computers store this as a *truncated approximation*. That tiny rounding
error is the source of famous bugs like
$$0.1 + 0.2 \neq 0.3$$
in nearly every programming language. The error is small (about
$10^{-17}$), but it accumulates in long calculations and matters for
financial software, scientific simulation, and ML training.

This is one reason **decimals (base 10) and binary (base 2) coexist**
in computing — currency code uses *fixed-point decimal* arithmetic to
avoid the rounding issue.
</details>

**S2.** What number does $0.\overline{9}$ (zero point nine recurring,
forever) equal?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Try this: let $x = 0.\overline{9}$. Compute $10x$. Then compute $10x - x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $x = 0.9999\ldots$. Then $10x = 9.9999\ldots$. Subtract:
$$10x - x = 9.9999\ldots - 0.9999\ldots = 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 9x = 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 1.$$

**$0.\overline{9} = 1$ exactly.** Same number, two costumes.

This is a famously *uncomfortable* result for many learners — but the
algebra is irrefutable. Acceptance of $0.\overline{9} = 1$ is acceptance
that the rationals (Lesson 7) extend continuously into the **real
numbers** — a story we tell properly in Tier 5.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"More decimal digits means a bigger number."** $0.5 > 0.45$, even
   though 0.5 has fewer digits. Compare column by column from the *left*,
   not by length.
2. **"Multiplying decimals always makes them smaller."** Only when both
   are less than 1. $1.5 \times 1.5 = 2.25$, which is bigger than either
   factor.
3. **"$0.\overline{9}$ is just *almost* 1."** No — it equals 1 exactly,
   as *Stretch S2* proved.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain why $0.05 < 0.5 < 0.55$ to a friend, using "column"
   and "place value"?
2. Without scrolling back, can you compute $1.4 + 0.85$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

- **3 yes:** ready for **[Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion](../09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (binary 0.1) — that's the deepest connection
  to systems work.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 7 — Fractions](../07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)** —
  decimals are a special form of fraction (denominators are powers of
  10).
- **[Lesson 3 — Place Value](../03-place-value/index.qmd)** — the dot
  just extends the columns rightward.

### What this unlocks

- **Ratio and Proportion** — percentages are decimals in a different
  costume (per hundred).
- **Tier 2 — Scientific Notation** — when the same number is huge or
  tiny, decimal place value combined with powers of 10 gives a compact
  form.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Systems.** *Stretch S1* is the most important takeaway: every
floating-point number on every computer is a binary approximation. The
rounding error inherent in this is the source of countless real-world
bugs — and is why financial systems use *decimal* arithmetic libraries
even when their programming language defaults to binary.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Almost every weight, activation, gradient,
and learning rate in a neural network is a floating-point decimal. The
*precision* of those decimals (32-bit, 16-bit, 8-bit) is now a
first-class design parameter for training large models.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** $0.\overline{9} = 1$ is the doorway to
**limits** and the structure of the real numbers — central topics of
Tier 5 real analysis.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Money, measurement, time (clocks have moved from
"hours and minutes" toward decimal hours), GPS coordinates, scientific
data — decimals are the world's lingua franca for non-whole quantities.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the number-line widget makes
  decimals literally movable), Boole (the trailing-zero question
  surfaces equivalence intuitively), Russian school (the long-division
  result for $1/3$ surprises and gets named, not skipped over),
  Bhāskara (Name + verse), 3Blue1Brown (the $0.\overline{9}=1$ proof
  is a classic visual-algebraic moment).
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
